Tolerance? Civility? Read the Platform
nytimes_articles.abstract:
There has been heated debate about Bob Dole's suggestion that the Republican  Party platform express "tolerance" and "civility" toward those who favor abortion rights. But what is to debate? 

     A statement of tolerance is but the smallest fig leaf and should fool no one. The platform is implacably anti-abortion and hostile to women's basic civil rights. For instance, the plank calls for a human life amendment, which would give the fetus full constitutional rights. This amendment would make it almost impossible for women to obtain legal abortions under any circumstances, including rape and incest.

wiki_events.headline: The Troubles: The Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) detonates a powerful truck bomb in the middle of Manchester, England, devastating the city centre and injuring 200 people.

pages.title: The Troubles
pages.abstract: The Troubles were an ethno-nationalist conflict in Northern Ireland that lasted for about 30 years from the late 1960s to 1998. Also known internationally as the Northern Ireland conflict, it began in the late 1960s and is usually deemed to have ended with the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Although the Troubles mostly took place in Northern Ireland, at times violence spilled over into parts of the Republic of Ireland, England, and mainland Europe.

pages.title: Provisional Irish Republican Army
pages.abstract: The Provisional Irish Republican Army, officially known as the Irish Republican Army and informally known as the Provos, was an Irish republican paramilitary force that sought to end British rule in Northern Ireland, facilitate Irish reunification and bring about an independent socialist republic, which would encompass all of Ireland. It was the most active republican paramilitary group during the Troubles. It argued that the all-island Irish Republic continued to exist, and it saw itself as that state's army, the sole legitimate successor to the original IRA from the Irish War of Independence. It was designated a terrorist organisation in the United Kingdom and an unlawful organisation in the Republic of Ireland, both of whose authority it rejected.

pages.title: 1996 Manchester bombing
pages.abstract: On 15 June 1996, the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) detonated a 1,500-kilogram (3,300 lb) lorry bomb on Corporation Street in the centre of Manchester, England. It was the biggest bomb detonated in Great Britain since the Second World War. It targeted the city's infrastructure and economy and caused significant damage, estimated by insurers at £700 million, a sum surpassed only by the 1993 Bishopsgate bombing, also by the IRA.

pages.title: Manchester city centre
pages.description: Central business district in Manchester, Greater Manchester, England
pages.abstract: Manchester city centre is the central business district of Manchester, England, within the confines of Great Ancoats Street, A6042 Trinity Way, and A57(M) Mancunian Way, which collectively form the inner ring road. The City Centre ward had a population of 17,861 at the 2011 census.

A new bill, currently wending its way through Congress, represents the first comprehensive national effort to deal with this emerging problem. Introduced last November by  Senators Robert F. nytimes_articles.abstract: IMMORTAL LOVE By Ludmilla Petrushevskaya. Translated by Sally Laird. 348 pp. New York: Pantheon Books. $25.     LYUDMILA PETRUSHEVSKAYA was born in Moscow in 1938, at the height of the Terror. Her father vanished when she was a baby, leaving her mother to support her during the war. When she was 9, she was sent to a children's home for a year, since her mother could not feed her. She was so thin, even among the emaciated orphans, that she earned the nickname moskvichka spichka -- the Moscow matchstick. Then came the privilege of sharing a mattress on a floor with her mother. She escaped these stifling conditions by going to libraries. Later she worked as a journalist and wrote plays and stories. Her first marriage ended tragically when her husband became paralyzed in an accident and then died, leaving her with a son to care for.

PAMELA TRACY, one of eight American Indian exchange students at Choate Rosemary Hall this spring, found the preparatory school's Wallingford campus undaunting. "They told us it was really big, but I think it's small," said the 18-year-old who was brought up on the Navajo Nation's 14.3 million acre reservation, the largest in the United States. 

     Ms. Tracy and seven other seniors at Window Rock High School in Window Rock,  Ariz., left their homes and families on the reservation to move into dorm rooms  at Choate, take classes, and get a taste of the Eastern boarding school experience.

VOICES: FROM THE DESK OF;The Lessons From Dad's Counter
nytimes_articles.abstract:
MY father, like his father before him, owned a small, retail store. Syd's Camera Shop was in White Plains, N.Y., on Main Street -- the proverbial, provincial, home-town thoroughfare where Dad did business in cozy proximity to the 18-karat chic of Bramley's Jewelers and the even-then anachronistic charms of Murray's Army and Navy. 

     Ours was a family business. My mother, who always did the books, advanced her career by attending a training school at Kodak. She returned with a diploma and  an unshakable faith in Kodak's products. My brother and I also worked in the store, following an employment code that was certainly genetic, though, truth to tell, my brother preferred to hang around the neighborhood gas station. He eventually grew up to become a psychologist.

CLASSICAL MUSIC;A New Era in the Care and Feeding of Artists
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN THE SCRAPBOOK OF MEMORIES shared by members of the classical-music industry, the artist manager Harold Shaw shows up in many guises: a triumphant impresario announcing that the pianist Vladimir Horowitz would join his roster and return to the concert stage in 1974 after a five-year absence; a devoted advance man tending to the considerable needs of the soprano Jessye Norman, another client; an urbane fixture in concerts halls and backstage areas for more than 40 years, known for his signature bow ties and berets. 

     But as the chairman of a major classical-music talent agency, he was not known for dropping bombshells. So it was doubly surprising to his staff, artists and colleagues when he announced last year that Shaw Concerts and its sister operation, Shaw Attractions, would cease operations as of this month. Mr. Shaw made the decision in May 1995, after learning that one of his top associates, Bill Capone, was leaving with 13 of the firm's clients in tow. "Rebuilding a roster takes about 10 years," the 73-year-old Mr. Shaw said recently. "At my age, I'd rather have a lot less responsibility."

nytimes_articles.abstract: LAND GIRLS By Angela Huth. 378 pp. New York: A Thomas Dunne Book/ St. Martin's Press. $23.95.     THE British novelist, playwright and journalist Angela Huth's lovely new book, "Land Girls," is a war story about the real heroes of World War II: not the generals but the ordinary citizens who left their workaday lives and fought  for their country as best they could. The girls of the title were young women who joined Britain's Land Army; they left the safe, tea-cozy world of traditional female jobs -- university student, hairdresser, sweetheart -- and went off to take up the exhausting, dawn-to-darkness labor of agricultural work  so the male farm workers could take up arms. At first blush, there is nothing exceptional about the three main characters. When gentle, cerebral Agatha, romantic Stella and fluff-brained Prue first assemble for their tour of duty on  the Lawrence farm in Dorset, the reader might be deceived into thinking this is  going to be yet another banal three-girls-meet-somewhere-and-become-friends novel, the sort in which there is inevitably one suicide, one ghastly abortion,  a slew of beastly men and a great display of passive aggression. But the reader  would be wrong; there is not one moment of melodrama in the entire book. And there is absolutely nothing banal about these women.

SCHOOLS;The High Cost of Senior Year
nytimes_articles.abstract:
A senior at Dickinson High School here confided to her guidance counselor last week that she would be unable to participate in the school's graduation ceremonies this Friday because she couldn't come up with the money to buy the cap and gown. 

     The guidance counselor took $25 out of her purse to cover the cost, a familiar practice among high school faculty members who don't want to see students miss out on one of the most important events of their young lives.

In Deep in Crete
nytimes_articles.abstract:
HALFWAY through our one-month trip to Greece and Turkey, my 7-year daughter,  Rachel, crossed her arms in front of her chest, came dangerously close to stamping her foot and, with the immediate complicity of her 10-year-old brother, Adam, wailed up into the blazing white Aegean sun, "Not another ruin! Why do you have to see another ruin? All they are is rocks!" 

     It was partly in anticipation of just such a reaction that my husband, Ernie, and I had planned to include a hike of the Samaria Gorge in western Crete at this point on our travels. Although we had been careful to balance strenuous sightseeing with beach time, after touring Athens, the Peloponnesus, Delphi, Mykonos, Delos and Santorini, we all felt the need for a change of pace, and the Samaria Gorge -- if the children could manage it -- promised to be it. Running approximately 10 miles from its start high in Crete's westernmost mountain range, the Levka Ori (White Mountains), to its end near the Mediterranean on the isolated south coast, the gorge is the longest mountain ravine in Europe.

You Can Look, But Ask First
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THESE days, when you don't have to be a queen (or know one) to get into Buckingham Palace, the hunt is on for anything in London still off-limits to the tourist crowds. 

     After a personal quest for some of the city's undiscovered attractions in April and October of last year, I can safely say, yes, there are sights that don't wear their "OPEN" hours on their gates and aren't subject to "Been There,  Done That" ho-hummery. In all cases, prior arrangement was necessary. Not every  visit knocked my socks off, of course. But as a whole the effort was worth it, providing a richer view of a city (and culture) I thought I already knew well.

Deconstructing the Bums
nytimes_articles.abstract: BROOKLYN'S DODGERS The Bums, the Borough, and the Best of Baseball, 1947-1957. By Carl E. Prince. Illustrated. 202 pp. New York: Oxford University Press. $23.    IT is enshrined in memory, the Brooklyn of the 1940's and 50's -- the object  of (not always so good-natured) derision, a kind of joke borough, source of an accent that, when reproduced ludicrously by outsiders, was unrecognized by its denizens. A joke borough of ferocious loyalties, it was a matrix and it was a necessary place from which to flee. Those of us who fled have never stopped singing its praises: the sweet butter from tubs, the pickles, charlotte russes (concoctions of sponge cake and strawberry mash and fresh whipped cream contained in a coronet of heavy white paper), warm Italian whole-wheat bread, unlocked front doors, civil cabdrivers (all of whom, if they were to be believed, were working to send their sons to Yale), lemon ices (a generic description that included chocolate) -- and the Brooklyn Dodgers, Dem Bums.

MAKING IT WORK;Greenpoint's Endangered Hampton
nytimes_articles.abstract:
GREENPOINT, Brooklyn, is a river-front neighborhood, but the only way to reach the river is to get past a set of squat warehouses at the water's edge, climb through unsanctioned holes in a razor-wire fence and walk onto a crumbling, concrete-covered pier. 

     This is the India Street Pier, where the regulars -- local people of Polish,  Italian and Puerto Rican descent -- sit on lawn chairs under a sun fixed in a building-free sky. Weekdays, a handful of regulars dot the pier; on the weekends, it is packed. Breezes from the water lap at hair, tickling sweaty scalps. Already, the regulars have turned a terra cotta color.

nytimes_articles.headline: A Survivor's Breast Cancer Stamp
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IN October 1993, Diane Sackett Nannery was given a diagnosis of breast cancer. Returning from the doctor's office to her house in Manorville, she slumped in front of the television set, "terrified out of my mind," she recalled, and began channel surfing. 

     "I'm flipping around mindlessly, and on one of these home shopping networks I see them advertising this Humpty Dumpty pin," she said. "I saw myself in that pin. All the king's horses ridden by all the best doctors in the world weren't going to put me back together again."

Backtalk;Innuendo 7, Coach 0
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One of the best college football coaches in the country, done in by shadows in Norman, Okla., can now be found sitting on his reputation in sunny Miami, waiting for a call and wondering what hit him. One year into a five-year coaching contract at the University of Oklahoma, Howard Schnellenberger was rendered jobless last December when his departure was announced as a "resignation." It was that only if you are willing to call a public hanging a suicide. 

     Schnellenberger was forced out at Oklahoma. But in the end, the rumors and innuendoes that spun around him in the last weeks of what he calls "the worst time of my life anywhere" say more about Oklahoma than the coach.

From the Rope to the Chair: 3 Centuries of Death
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WHEN it comes to capital punishment, justice, such as it was, could be swift  two centuries ago in New Jersey. On a December day in 1731, a man named Garrett  Hoppe of Bergen County accused a slave named Harry of threatening him. The next  day, Harry was put to death. 

     There is no record of a trial, a defense, a review or an appeal of whether Mr. Hoppe's hurt feelings were worth another man's life.
